items.q: What do orthodox Christians believe about the nature of Jesus?
items.correct: He is fully God and fully human at the same time (the incarnation) — both natures united in one person, as defined at the Council of Chalcedon.
items.misconception: Jesus was half God and half human.
items.random_wrong: Jesus was a human who became God after his resurrection.
items.why: The 'half and half' model was historically rejected as heresy. Orthodox doctrine holds both natures fully and simultaneously — a deliberately paradoxical claim students often soften.

items.q: Why do Jews not accept Jesus as the Messiah?
items.correct: Because the Jewish Messiah is expected to bring peace, rebuild the Temple and restore Israel — events the Jewish tradition holds Jesus did not accomplish.
items.misconception: Because Jews believe Jesus never existed.
items.random_wrong: Because Jesus was crucified, and the Messiah cannot die.
items.why: Most Jewish thinkers accept Jesus existed as a historical figure. The disagreement is about whether he fulfilled messianic prophecies — a theological judgement, not denial of history.

items.q: What do Christians mean by resurrection, and how does it differ from reincarnation?
items.correct: Resurrection is the once-for-all bodily raising to eternal life at the end of time (modelled on Christ's resurrection); reincarnation is the soul's repeated rebirth into new bodies. They are distinct doctrines from different religious traditions.
items.misconception: Resurrection and reincarnation are basically the same idea of life after death.
items.random_wrong: Christians believe everyone is reincarnated until they accept Jesus.
items.why: Christianity affirms one bodily resurrection, not cyclical rebirth. Students flatten different afterlife concepts into a single 'life after death' idea, missing the very different metaphysics involved.

items.q: How many books are in the Roman Catholic Bible?
items.why: Correct answer: 73. “66” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which Greek word describes the Second Coming of Christ?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Parousia. “Pentecost” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many books are in the Protestant Bible?
items.why: The real figure is 66. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
